topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the shared apartment had become a heavy, suffocating blanket. Liam felt perpetually weighted down, every movement an effort against an invisible resistance. He’d been struggling with a relentless wave of low energy lately, a persistent fog that clung to him even after sleep. One Tuesday afternoon, while searching for a misplaced textbook, he noticed the journal lying open on his desk. It wasn't his. His roommate, Mark, had been reading it. Liam’s stomach tightened into a knot. He approached slowly, his steps feeling clumsy. "Hey, Mark," he managed, his voice thin. Mark looked up, a practiced, slightly too-bright smile on his face. "Oh, this? Just curious about what someone else is writing." The casualness of the statement sent a jolt of icy dread through Liam. He felt his shoulders slump further, the effort of maintaining composure draining him entirely.

[story 2]

My eyelids felt like lead weights. Getting out of bed was an Olympic event. I spent most of my mornings just existing, a slow, viscous movement through the apartment. Then, the realization hit—a cold, sharp spike of panic. My diary, tucked under my pillow, was open. Mark’s handwriting filled the pages, his pen hovering over the ink, his gaze fixed intently on the script. I froze, my breath catching in my throat. I couldn't move. My hands felt strangely numb, heavy, as if they were submerged in water. When Mark finally looked up, his expression shifted from casual interest to something guarded, something that made the air in the room feel thin and brittle. I just stared at the floor, wishing the floor would swallow me whole.

[story 3]

The fluorescent lights of the common room seemed too harsh, amplifying the dull ache that had settled deep in my bones. I hadn't eaten properly in two days, and the effort required to simply sit upright felt monumental. When I finally ventured near my desk, I saw it—the open book. It was mine, my private confessions and half-formed dreams laid bare. And Mark was there, leaning in close, his attention completely absorbed by the words. A profound sense of exposure washed over me, leaving a hollow space where my usual anxiety used to reside. My jaw felt slack, my eyes unfocused, unable to process the betrayal. I just stood there, a statue carved from exhaustion and shock.
